text.title: Technical Debt Concepts
text.summary: Fowler's debt quadrant, six debt types, and the interest-cost framing that makes debt legible to stakeholders.

rule.statement: Classify every debt item on two axes: deliberate vs inadvertent, and reckless vs prudent — only then decide whether to register it.
rule.rationale: Not all debt is equal; prudent-deliberate debt is a legitimate trade-off; reckless-anything is a red flag for code review.

list.item: Deliberate + Reckless: "We don't have time for design" — HIGH RISK; block in code review.
Deliberate + Prudent: "We must ship now and deal with consequences" — STRATEGIC; log and schedule payoff.
Inadvertent + Reckless: "What's layering?" — INCOMPETENCE; fix with training and review.
Inadvertent + Prudent: "Now we know how we should have done it" — LEARNING; log and pay when convenient.
Code Debt: messy code, duplication, poor naming.
Design Debt: poor architecture, tight coupling, violated SRP.
Test Debt: missing tests, flaky tests, tests without assertions.
Documentation Debt: outdated or missing docs, stale ADRs.
Infrastructure Debt: outdated dependencies, EOL runtimes, legacy deployment scripts.
Process Debt: missing CI/CD, manual deployments, no code review.

antipattern.description: Conflating code smells with debt: every TODO or long function gets flagged as high-severity debt.
antipattern.reason: Without a business-impact filter the register becomes noise; require concrete evidence (incident IDs, churn data, slow-query logs).

rule.statement: Each registered debt item must include an interest estimate: hours/week or hours/month of extra work caused by its presence.
rule.rationale: Interest cost converts abstract code quality into concrete business cost; enables stakeholder prioritization without technical jargon.

rule.statement: Require evidence for the interest estimate: incident IDs, PR latency data, slow-query traces, or churn statistics — no invented numbers.
rule.rationale: Agents default to high severity with confident numbers; without evidence the estimates are noise that erodes trust.
